topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence of the locker room was a physical weight pressing down on Elias. He traced the faded lettering on his jersey, the once-vibrant colors now seeming dull and mocking. The air smelled stale, thick with sweat and unspoken disappointment. He watched the other hopefuls laughing, their easy camaraderie a sharp contrast to the knot tightening in his chest. When the final roster was announced, his name wasn't there. A hollow ache settled deep in his stomach, a cold, heavy sensation that made his limbs feel sluggish. He managed a weak nod, the movement feeling monumental, before retreating to the empty bench, the sound of his own ragged breathing echoing too loudly in the quiet space.

[story 2]

"Just another dead end," Sarah muttered, kicking at a loose piece of scuffed floor. Her shoulders slumped, pulling her arms tight across her chest as if trying to physically contain the churning inside her. She stared at the polished floor, seeing only reflections of her own inadequacy. The weight of the rejection settled on her like a shroud, making it hard to draw a full breath. She picked up her water bottle, the plastic cool against her clammy fingers, and took a long, shaky sip. The taste was metallic, mirroring the sourness rising in her throat. She didn't look up, focusing instead on the way the fluorescent lights hummed, a monotonous, irritating sound that matched the dull throb behind her eyes.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the final practice in my head—the missed pass, the clumsy tackle. Every memory looped, sharp and unforgiving, landing squarely in the pit of my gut. When the coach finally called the names, the sound was distant, muffled, like hearing something underwater. My hands, usually steady and strong, trembled as I walked toward the exit. The hallway felt endless, the beige walls pressing in. I leaned against a cold metal doorframe, letting my head drop until my forehead rested against the cool surface. The world outside seemed too bright, too loud, and I felt utterly disconnected, a muted observer in my own life.
